frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: sensory precision allocated according to learned stimulus statistics

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: average-feature inference combining prior with efficient-coding-shaped likelihood

frameworks.name: robust averaging
frameworks.one_line: inlier-biased ensemble weighting reframed as consequence of encoding, not heuristic

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: orientation discrimination relative to a reference element

core_question: Can robust averaging in ensemble perception be explained by a Bayesian observer constrained by efficient sensory coding?
key_assumptions: observer combines prior with likelihood via Bayes rule
sensory encoding precision is optimized to learned stimulus statistics
stimulus statistics can be acquired on short experimental timescales
manipulations: element feature distance from the mean (inlying vs outlying)
reference element fixed vs varied within blocks
Gaussian distribution mean and variance of the element features
key_findings: higher weights for inlying than outlying elements
overall higher weights when the reference was fixed within blocks
higher discrimination accuracy when the distribution had a large generic mean and small variance
model also reproduced robust-averaging signatures across color and shape features
interpretations: robust averaging arises from inhomogeneous encoding precision of inlying vs outlying elements
efficient sensory representations can be established short-term by learning experimental stimulus statistics
